request.question: Which mountain range, found in eastern North America, was seen as the western frontier prior to about 1800?

title: Hemlock woolly adelgid
Appalachian Mountains
Coast Mountains
Front Range
Bashagard Mountains
Baffin Mountains
Amanita bisporigera
Western United States
Piedmont Mountains
Geological history of the Precordillera terrane
sentences: Hemlock woolly adelgid ("Adelges tsugae"), or HWA, is member of the Sternorrhyncha suborder of the Order Hemiptera and native to East Asia.
It feeds by sucking sap from hemlock and spruce trees ("Tsuga" spp.; "Picea" spp.)
In eastern North America, it is a destructive pest that gravely threatens the eastern hemlock ("Tsuga canadensis") and the Carolina hemlock ("Tsuga caroliniana").
Though the range of eastern hemlock extends north of the current range of the adelgid, it could spread to infect these northern areas as well.
Accidentally introduced to North America from Japan, HWA was first found in the eastern United States near Richmond, Virginia, in the early 1950s.
The pest has now been established in eighteen eastern states from Georgia to Massachusetts, causing widespread mortality of hemlock trees.
As of 2015, 90% of the geographic range of eastern hemlock in North America has been impacted by HWA.
The Appalachian Mountains ( , French: "les Appalaches" ), often called the Appalachians, are a system of mountains in eastern North America.
The Appalachians first formed roughly 480 million years ago during the Ordovician Period.
It once reached elevations similar to those of the Alps and the Rocky Mountains before naturally occurring erosion.
The Appalachian chain is a barrier to east-west travel, as it forms a series of alternating ridgelines and valleys oriented in opposition to most roads running east or west.

The Coast Mountains are a major mountain range in the Pacific Coast Ranges of western North America, extending from southwestern Yukon through the Alaska Panhandle and virtually all of the Coast of British Columbia south to the Fraser River.
The mountain range's name derives from its proximity to the sea coast, and it is often referred to as the Coast Range.
The range includes volcanic and non-volcanic mountains and the extensive ice fields of the Pacific and Boundary Ranges, and the northern end of the volcanic system known as the Cascade Volcanoes.

The Coast Mountains are part of a larger mountain system called the Pacific Coast Ranges or the Pacific Mountain System, which includes the Cascade Range, the Insular Mountains, the Olympic Mountains, the Oregon Coast Range, the California Coast Ranges, the Saint Elias Mountains and the Chugach Mountains.

The Coast Mountains are also part of the American Cordilleraa Spanish term for an extensive chain of mountain rangesthat consists of an almost continuous sequence of mountain ranges that form the western backbone of North America, Central America, South America and Antarctica.

The Front Range is a mountain range of the Southern Rocky Mountains of North America located in the central portion of the U.S. State of Colorado, and southeastern portion of the U.S. State of Wyoming.
It is the first mountain range encountered moving west along the 40th parallel north across the Great Plains of North America.
The Front Range runs north-south between Casper, Wyoming and Pueblo, Colorado and rises nearly 10,000 feet above the Great Plains.
Longs Peak, Mount Evans, and Pikes Peak are its most prominent peaks, visible from the Interstate 25 corridor.
The area is a popular destination for mountain biking, hiking, climbing, and camping during the warmer months and for skiing and snowboarding during winter.
Millions of years ago the present-day Front Range was home to ancient mountain ranges, deserts, beaches, and even oceans.
The Bashagard Mountains or the Bashagerd Mountains (also known as Bashagird or Bashakerd) is a mountain range that is located in southeast of Iran.

The mountain range runs in an arc almost in a northwest-southeast direction from eastern parts of Hormozgan Province along the border with Kerman Province and stretching into southern part of Sistan and Baluchestan Province.

With an elevation of 2185 metres, Mount Buniken (Kuh-e Buniken) is the highest point of the range located in the western section of the Bashagard Mountains, east of Sardasht in Bashagard County in Hormozgan Province, and in the vicinity of a place that marks the borders of Kerman, Hormozgan, and Sistan and Baluchestan provinces.
Made of ophiolite, an assemblage of mafic igneous rocks representing remnants of former oceanic crust, the mountain range was formed finally in the Miocene and the Pliocene during the Alpine orogeny.
The mountain range is located in a region with a hot semi-desert climate.
The Baffin Mountains are a mountain range running along the northeastern coast of Baffin Island and Bylot Island, Nunavut are part of the Arctic Cordillera.
The ice-capped mountains are some of the highest peaks of eastern North America, reaching a height of 1525 - above sea level.

While they could be considered a single mountain range as they are separated by bodies of water to make Baffin Island, this is not true, as they are closely related to the other mountain ranges that make the much larger Arctic Cordillera mountain range.
Amanita bisporigera is a deadly poisonous species of fungus in the family Amanitaceae.

It is commonly known as the eastern North American destroying angel or just as the destroying angel, although the fungus shares this latter name with three other lethal white "Amanita" species, "A. ocreata", "A. verna" and "A. virosa".

The fruit bodies are found on the ground in mixed coniferous and deciduous forests of eastern North America south to Mexico, but are rare in western North America; the fungus has also been found in pine plantations in Colombia.
The mushroom has a smooth white cap that can reach up to 10 cm across, and a stipe, up to 14 cm long by 1.8 cm thick, that has a delicate white skirt-like ring near the top.
The bulbous stipe base is covered with a membranous sac-like volva.
The white gills are free from attachment to the stalk and crowded closely together.
As the species name suggests, "A. bisporigera" typically bears two spores on the basidia, although this characteristic is not as immutable as was once thought.
The Western United States, commonly referred to as the American West, the Far West, or simply the West, traditionally refers to the region comprising the westernmost states of the United States.
Because European settlement in the U.S. expanded westward after its founding, the meaning of the West has evolved over time.
Prior to about 1800, the crest of the Appalachian Mountains was seen as the western frontier.
The Piedmont Mountains are outlying mountains, sometimes called “low mountains”, that typically occur in the western Piedmont near the Blue Ridge.

Most of the features within the Piedmont physiographic region of North America lie either on the eastern border where the plateau plunges onto the Coastal Plain at the Fall Line, in the broad valleys of the river systems, or on the western border where Piedmont Mountains likely occur.

Occasionally, due to diverse rock formations, folds and outcroppings, these mountains can rise at various locations across the Piedmont like the Uwharrie Mountain Range in North Carolina or the Pine Mountain Range in Georgia.
Most of these mountains, or hills, are what is left of ancient eroded mountains.
Some, like Stone Mountain in Georgia, are solitary rock domes called Monadnocks which become further exposed with erosion.
The Piedmont is part of the greater Appalachian Mountain Range and is also referred to as the Appalachian Plateau.
The French definition of piedmont in itself translates as foothill; however, a Piedmont Mountain may be that of greater significance or prominent elevation.
The Precordillera terrane of western Argentina is a large mountain range located southeast of the main Andes mountain range.
The evolution of the Precordillera is noted for its unique formation history compared to the region nearby.

The Cambrian-Ordovian sedimentology in the Precordillera terrane has its source neither from old Andes nor nearby country rock, but shares similar characteristics with the Grenville orogeny of eastern North America.
This indicates a rift-drift history of the Precordillera in the early Paleozoic.
The Precordillera is a moving micro-continent which started from the southeast part of the ancient continent Laurentia (current location: North American plate).
The separation of the Precordillera (also named Cuyania) started around the early Cambrian.
The mass collided with Gondwana (the ancient supercontinent in the southern hemisphere) around Late Ordovician period.
Different models and thinking of rift-drift process and the time of occurrence have been proposed.
This page focuses on the evidence of drifting found in the stratigraphical record of the Precordillera, as well as exhibiting models of how the Precordillera drifted to Gondwana.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here. Emit exactly one field named `summary`.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A one-line header naming the primary topic(s) identified in the passages.
- 1–4 short lines enumerating concrete facts directly supported by the passages. Extract entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Do not add or infer facts not present in the passages.
- An explicit statement that names the missing bridge fact or relation needed for the next hop (express in abstract role terms, e.g., "missing bridge fact: [relation] between [candidate entity] and [target attribute]"). If passages supply candidate bridge entities but do not confirm the required relation, label them as "possible bridge entity" and state what is unverified for each.
- 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (highest priority first). Each directive must be concrete and section-aware: specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Early life", "History"), or tight search queries/keywords. Prefer section-level targets and tight searches over broad suggestions.

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` compact: one-line header plus a total of 1–4 brief lines (sentences or bullet lines) covering the factual spans, the missing bridge fact, and the prioritized retrieval directives.
- Preserve exact factual spans from the passages; when quoting, keep quotes very short (<= ~15 words) and only if verbatim in the passages.
- Do not assert a final answer or resolve facts here. If the passages do fully and unambiguously contain the required relation, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but still do not produce a final answer.
- If multiple candidate entities or variant names appear, enumerate the top 1–2 candidates and mark the name as ambiguous, stating what is unverified for each.
- Do not invent facts, hypothesize, or add extraneous context not grounded in the provided passages.

Output expectation: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary as specified above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

When composing the query, select the minimal anchor token(s) visible in `question` and/or `summary_1` that point to the missing page or fact. You may append a single short attribute keyword (for example country member occupations record season) only when that keyword is needed to disambiguate the evidence type. Do not introduce any other new tokens.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Use these operational rules when choosing a template:
- If summary_1 explicitly lists two or more distinct page anchors as next-hop targets, combine those visible anchor tokens into one compact multi-anchor query and append a short attribute keyword naming the evidence type (for example country member occupations record).
- If summary_1 flags a single missing anchor or a missing_titles list is present, emit a single-anchor metadata-style query that places the missing anchor token first and appends a concise attribute keyword naming the desired fact.
- When summary_1 names multiple candidate entities and the missing fact concerns one candidate’s membership, order, or relation to a canonical collection, prefer a connector-style query that pairs the highest-priority candidate token (first or prioritized in summary_1) with the canonical collection token plus a short attribute keyword requesting membership or order.
- When individual member tokens are present but the needed retrieval is about that individual, prefer member-token + group-anchor + member (or equivalent short attribute) to target the individual’s page rather than issuing a broad group-level query.

Priority rules for ambiguity and disambiguation:
- If summary_1 lists multiple candidate anchors or otherwise signals ambiguity, emit a single connector-style query that concatenates the prioritized anchor tokens (use the order or priority visible in summary_1) and then append exactly one short attribute token or a visible year token for disambiguation.
- If summary_1 contains a comma-separated list of individuals and the missing evidence concerns a member, prefer the earliest-listed individual token paired with the visible group token and append the single attribute token member.
- If the question contains a visible year token that disambiguates a title, include that year token before the attribute token in the query.
- If none of the above applies, emit a single-anchor query using the most relevant visible token and at most one short attribute token when the question implies an attribute.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Additional constraints (must follow):
- The first sentence must be an answer-first concise identification (a direct answer or concise identification). If the `context` already contains such a sentence, preserve it exactly unless a passage explicitly and directly contradicts it; otherwise derive that first sentence from the clearest passage.
- After the first sentence, append at most one short supporting sentence for each additional relevant named entity only when the passages supply corroborating attributes. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the inputs (prefer the form used in the context when there is a name-variant conflict) and paraphrase no more than one or two simple attributes.
- When a queried binary/status attribute for an entity is not stated in the provided passages, indicate that absence concisely within that entity's supporting sentence (for example, with a short parenthetical or clause saying the status is not given in the provided evidence).
- If the passages explicitly assert a relationship between two or more entities that is directly relevant to the question, include one short relationship sentence after the per-entity supporting sentence(s) describing that relationship; do not embed the same relationship claim redundantly in multiple sentences.
- If relevant entities exceed the available supporting-sentence budget under the 1–3 sentence limit, prioritize entities in this order: entities explicitly named in the question, entities named in the preserved first sentence, then other context entities. Do not exceed the 1–3 sentence cap.

Follow these additional concise operational rules when integrating evidence:
- Prefer the context-preferred name form for the preserved first (answer) sentence; when choosing a canonical name for supporting sentences, prefer the context form if there is a name-variant conflict across inputs.
- If a passage explicitly contradicts a preserved context sentence, replace the preserved sentence with a passage-supported first sentence or note the contradiction concisely in that first sentence.
- For each supporting sentence, start with the chosen canonical name and include only attributes explicitly present in the provided passages; if the passages do not assert a requested status or attribute, state that absence briefly rather than inferring the attribute.
- Do not add fields, requests for more retrieval, or change the input/output interface. Keep the `summary` to 1–3 sentences exactly and avoid extra commentary or formatting.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add any new facts or external information.
- First run a mandatory binary detection: determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Do not infer that the final state is unresolved merely because `summary_1` uses earlier unresolved or bridge language; prefer `summary_2` when it clarifies `summary_1`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

Binary detection must run first. Only if the question is not a binary yes/no or the binary detector does not apply, proceed to extraction.

Conservative extractive tie-breaking (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- When a contiguous longer verbatim phrase in either summary strictly contains a shorter candidate (adds adjacent words that are part of the name or label), emit the longest contiguous verbatim phrase exactly as it appears in the summaries.
- For personal names where one verbatim form is a substring of another, prefer the longest verbatim personal-name span found in either summary; if multiple distinct spans tie for length, prefer the form from `summary_2`.
- If either summary presents an alternate token parenthetically immediately adjacent to a name/place (e.g., canonical short form in parentheses), emit that parenthetical token exactly as shown. If both summaries present parenthetical tokens, prefer the one in `summary_2`.
- If the summaries do not explicitly state the requested relation but exactly one candidate entity of the relevant role appears verbatim across the summaries and no summary contradicts the attribution, emit that single verbatim entity. If multiple distinct candidates appear or there is any contradiction, do not apply this rule and return the unresolved outcome.
- Prefer wording from `summary_2` only as a tie-breaker, except when the longest-contiguous-span or parenthetical-token rules require choosing a longer or parenthetical form present in `summary_1`.

Extraction and formatting rules (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- Person-name selection: if a longer verbatim personal-name string appears in either summary and contains a shorter candidate as a substring, emit the longest contiguous verbatim name exactly as it appears in the summaries. Prefer wording from `summary_2` when both summaries provide the same evidence but differ in form.
- Denomination/type extraction: for questions asking a "kind", "type", or "denomination" of a role, extract and emit exactly the minimal token or short phrase that appears immediately adjacent to the role word in the summaries (the word/phrase answering "what kind"). If multiple adjacent phrases appear, prefer the longest contiguous phrase unless that creates contradiction.
- Event-name extraction: when summaries show a contiguous proper-name followed by a generic event-type noun, emit only the contiguous proper-name core. Strip any leading articles and remove the trailing generic event-type noun; emit only the proper-name portion exactly as given in the summaries.
- Nationality handling: if both summaries unambiguously state the same nationality phrase and at least one summary places the literal single word "citizen" immediately after that nationality phrase, append exactly one space and the single word "citizen" to the nationality phrase. Otherwise emit the nationality phrase exactly as given in the summaries.

General formatting constraints for all outputs:
- Always emit the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries and do not add explanation, context, punctuation, or extra words.
- Do not introduce external facts, additional descriptors, dates, roles, or explanatory text not present in the summaries.
- Do not normalize or invent name variants that are not verbatim in the summaries.
- If the summaries conflict, lack the required adjacent phrasing, or do not present a clear verbatim form for the requested attribute, do not guess—use the module's normal unresolved output.

Output contract:
- Produce a single field `answer` whose contents follow the rules above. The `answer` field must contain only the minimal string supported by `summary_1` and `summary_2` and no additional tokens, punctuation, or explanation.

Use only the provided summaries as evidence and follow the conservative tie-breaking rules above when extracting the final minimal verbatim span.

response.hop1_query: Which mountain range, found in eastern North America, was seen as the western frontier prior to about 1800?
hop1_docs: Western United States | The Western United States, commonly referred to as the American West, the Far West, or simply the West, traditionally refers to the region comprising the westernmost states of the United States.  Because European settlement in the U.S. expanded westward after its founding, the meaning of the West has evolved over time.  Prior to about 1800, the crest of the Appalachian Mountains was seen as the western frontier.

Chuska Mountains | The Chuska Mountains are an elongate range on the southeast Colorado Plateau and within the Navajo Nation whose highest elevations approach 10,000 feet.  The range is about 80 by 15 km (50 by 10 miles).  It trends north-northwest and is crossed by the state line between Arizona and New Mexico.  The highlands are a dissected plateau, with an average elevation of about 2740 m , and subdued topography.  The highest point is Roof Butte (36.4601° N, 109.0929° W) at 2994 m , near the northern end of the range in Arizona.  Other high points include the satellite Beautiful Mountain at 2861 m and Lukachukai Mountains at 2885 m , both also near the northern end, and Matthews Peak at 2911 m .  The San Juan Basin borders the Chuskas on the east, and typical elevations in nearby parts of that basin are near 1800 m .  The eastern escarpment of the mountains is marked by slumps and landslides that extend out onto the western margin of the San Juan Basin.  To the north, the Chuskas are separated from the Carrizo Mountains by Red Rock Valley, which is today commonly referred to as Red Valley.

Rocky Mountain elk | The Rocky Mountain elk ("Cervus canadensis nelsoni") is a subspecies of elk found in the Rocky Mountains and adjacent ranges of Western North America.  The winter ranges are most common in open forests and floodplain marshes in the lower elevations.  In the summer it migrates to the subalpine forests and alpine basins.  The total wild population is about one million individuals.

American Cordillera | The American Cordillera is a chain of mountain ranges (cordilleras) that consists of an almost continuous sequence of mountain ranges that form the western "backbone" of North America, Central America, South America and Antarctica.  It is also the backbone of the volcanic arc that forms the eastern half of the Pacific Ring of Fire.

Coast Mountains | The Coast Mountains are a major mountain range in the Pacific Coast Ranges of western North America, extending from southwestern Yukon through the Alaska Panhandle and virtually all of the Coast of British Columbia south to the Fraser River.  The mountain range's name derives from its proximity to the sea coast, and it is often referred to as the Coast Range.  The range includes volcanic and non-volcanic mountains and the extensive ice fields of the Pacific and Boundary Ranges, and the northern end of the volcanic system known as the Cascade Volcanoes.  The Coast Mountains are part of a larger mountain system called the Pacific Coast Ranges or the Pacific Mountain System, which includes the Cascade Range, the Insular Mountains, the Olympic Mountains, the Oregon Coast Range, the California Coast Ranges, the Saint Elias Mountains and the Chugach Mountains.  The Coast Mountains are also part of the American Cordilleraa Spanish term for an extensive chain of mountain rangesthat consists of an almost continuous sequence of mountain ranges that form the western backbone of North America, Central America, South America and Antarctica.

Rhododendron occidentale | Rhododendron occidentale, the western azalea or California azalea, is one of two deciduous "Rhododendron" species native to western North America (the other is "Rhododendron albiflorum").  The western azalea is known to occur as far north as Lincoln and Douglas Counties in Oregon and as far south as the mountains of San Diego county.  Typically found in the coastal ranges of western North America, it also grows in the Cascade and Sierra Nevada mountain ranges, but is not known east of them.

Arbutus xalapensis | Arbutus xalapensis, commonly known as the Texas madrone, naked Indian tree or Texas madroño, is a species of flowering plant in the heather family.  It is native to Central America, the southwestern United States (western Texas and New Mexico), and throughout Mexico.  It is found in canyons and mountains, on rocky plains, and in oak woodlands, at altitudes of up to 3,000 m (10,000 feet) in the south of the range, but lower, down to 600 m (1800 feet) in the north of the range.
response.summary_1:
Primary topic: Appalachian Mountains

- Passage [1]: "the crest of the Appalachian Mountains" was seen as the "western frontier" prior to about 1800.
- No missing bridge fact — passage [1] supplies the required relation unambiguously; no additional retrieval needed.
- Optional verification (low priority): fetch "Appalachian Mountains" — lede/infobox and "History" or "Geography" sections, or search "Appalachian Mountains western frontier prior to 1800" for corroboration.
hop2_docs: Western United States | The Western United States, commonly referred to as the American West, the Far West, or simply the West, traditionally refers to the region comprising the westernmost states of the United States.  Because European settlement in the U.S. expanded westward after its founding, the meaning of the West has evolved over time.  Prior to about 1800, the crest of the Appalachian Mountains was seen as the western frontier.

Overmountain Men | The Overmountain Men were American frontiersmen from west of the Appalachian Mountains who took part in the American Revolutionary War.  While they were present at multiple engagements in the war's southern campaign, they are best known for their role in the American victory at the Battle of Kings Mountain in 1780.  The term "overmountain" refers to the fact that their settlements were west of, or "over", the Appalachians —the range being the primary geographical boundary dividing the 13 American colonies from the western frontier.  The Overmountain Men hailed from parts of Virginia, North Carolina, and what is now Tennessee and Kentucky.

The Forgotten Frontier | The Forgotten Frontier (1931) is a documentary film about the Frontier Nursing Service, nurses on horseback, who traveled the back roads of the Appalachian Mountains of eastern Kentucky.  It was directed by Mary Marvin Breckinridge, and featured her cousin, Mary Breckinridge, who was a nurse-midwife and founded the Frontier Nursing Service.  Also featured are the people of Leslie County, Kentucky, many of whom reenacted their stories.

List of mountains of the Alleghenies | This list of ridges and summits of the Allegheny Mountains identifies geographic elevations for about 500 miles (800 km) from north central Pennsylvania, through eastern West Virginia and western Maryland, to western Virginia in the USA.  The range of the Allegheny Mountains is part of the Ridge-and-Valley Appalachians, a physiographic region of the much larger Appalachian Mountain Range.

Appalachian Plateau | The Appalachian Plateau is a series of rugged, high plains located on the western side of the Appalachian Highlands.  The Appalachian Highlands are a mountain range that run down the entire east coast of the United States.

Appalachian Ski Mountain | Appalachian Ski Mountain is a ski area in the eastern United States, one of three ski mountains in the Watauga County area of western North Carolina.  It is located off of US 321 between Blowing Rock and Boone.

Battle for the Old Mountain Jug | The Battle for the Old Mountain Jug was an American college football rivalry game played between the Mountaineers of Appalachian State University and the Catamounts of Western Carolina University.  The rivalry went dormant when Appalachian State moved the Sun Belt Conference after the 2013 season.
hop2_titles: List of mountains of the Alleghenies
Battle for the Old Mountain Jug
response.summary_2: "the crest of the Appalachian Mountains" was seen as the "western frontier" prior to about 1800. Appalachian Mountains are a mountain range in eastern North America that served as the primary geographic boundary dividing the original 13 colonies from the western frontier.
